sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Minor injuries include superficial wounds, abrasions, minor lacerations, contusions, sprains, and simple fractures that do not immediately threaten life or limb. Epistaxis is usually anterior and self-limiting, but ongoing bleeding, posterior bleeding, anticoagulation, or associated trauma may increase risk.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
**Minor injuries:**
- Localised pain, swelling, bruising, tenderness, or reduced movement
- Superficial bleeding or small lacerations
- Mild deformity in uncomplicated fractures

**Epistaxis:**
- Bleeding from one or both nostrils
- Blood in the throat or mouth
- Swallowing blood, nausea, or vomiting
- Dizziness or signs of significant blood loss in more severe cases

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Use aseptic technique for open wounds
- Consider anticoagulant or antiplatelet use, recurrent bleeding, recent trauma, posterior bleeding, or coagulopathy
- Posterior epistaxis may bleed more than is externally visible
- Do not pack both nostrils unless advanced airway control and senior support are available

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey and focused Vital Sign Survey
- Control wound bleeding with direct pressure and dress appropriately
- Clean wounds with sterile water or saline and remove visible superficial debris only
- Splint simple fractures and support sprains or strains with rest, compression, elevation, or intermittent cooling as appropriate
- For epistaxis, sit the patient upright and leaning forward, discourage swallowing blood, and apply continuous pressure to the soft part of the nose for at least 10 minutes
- Consider simple analgesia where appropriate and avoid NSAIDs during active bleeding

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider Steri-Strips, wound adhesive, or suturing for suitable wounds within scope
- If epistaxis persists after correct direct pressure, consider a topical vasoconstrictor or anterior nasal packing within scope and local protocol
- Consider topical tranexamic acid as an adjunct for persistent anterior epistaxis only after initial pressure measures have failed and where local protocol or senior direction supports its use
- Escalate early if bleeding continues, posterior epistaxis is suspected, the patient is anticoagulated, or there are signs of haemodynamic compromise

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Enquire about tetanus status where relevant
- Provide prevention advice for recurrent anterior epistaxis such as humidification, gentle nose care, and avoidance of digital trauma
